eyebrow: If I Awaken in Los Angeles · Space 06 of 06
title: The Stage
summary: After five immersive neighborhood environments, audiences arrive at the only space in the show that looks like a theater. A classical proscenium — matte black frame, warm walnut deck, black velour with a white scrim reveal — sits inside a raw warehouse shell. The tour guide opens solo; the company gathers beat by beat; a full-ensemble finale lands before a flat-line bow.
callout:
Audience exits Space 5 through the NE corner and ramps down into the mid-house cross-aisle of the amphitheater. Three rows descend ahead of them toward the stage (R1 at –2 ft, in the old dock pit); five rows ascend behind them to R8 at +32". Every seat is within ~30 ft of the stage edge. The stage itself is deliberately neutral — a matte black proscenium wraps the existing steel columns into a 44 × 24 ft portal, the deck is warm walnut hardwood raised 2 ft over the dock pit, a full-width black velour traveler closes the back with a white bobbinet scrim waiting behind it for the finale. Nothing on the stage references LA or any earlier space.

The design move is the contrast: a pristine classical stage sitting inside a raw brick warehouse, brick walls left exposed on both audience sides. The seven-beat arc runs tour guide solo → band arrives → poets arrive → dancers arrive → final number (velour opens, scrim ignites) → bow. The scrim reveal is Space 6's only capital-R reveal — everything else is restraint so that reveal can land.

          <text x="78" y="591" fill="#8a6a48" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="8" text-anchor="end">R8</text>

          <!-- Entry arrow from Space 5 SW -->
          <path d="M 10 610 Q 30 530 75 435" stroke="#d4a24c" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#s6arrow)" opacity="0.85" fill="none"></path>
          <text x="5" y="590" fill="#d4a24c" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="9" letter-spacing="1.5" text-anchor="start">FROM SPACE 5</text>
          <text x="5" y="604" fill="#d4a24c" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="7" letter-spacing="1" text-anchor="start" opacity="0.8">RAMP TO CROSS-AISLE</text>

          <!-- Dimension callouts -->
          <g opacity="0.6">
            <line x1="40" y1="30" x2="600" y2="30" stroke="#a89b82" stroke-width="0.5"></line>
            <text x="320" y="22" fill="#a89b82" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="9" letter-spacing="1" text-anchor="middle">45 FT</text>
          </g>
          <g opacity="0.6">
            <line x1="28" y1="40" x2="28" y2="600" stroke="#a89b82" stroke-width="0.5"></line>
            <text x="18" y="320" fill="#a89b82" font-family="JetBrains Mono" font-size="9" letter-spacing="1" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 18 320)">55 FT</text>
          </g>
        </svg>

legend.label: Stage
legend.note: +2 ft over dock pit · 19 ft deep · walnut

legend.label: Velour + wings
legend.note: Black traveler · scrim behind · SL/SR legs

legend.label: ADA
legend.note: 4 WC + 4 companion · R5 upper + R8 back

beats.title: Arrival · finding seats
beats.body: Audience exits Space 5, ramps down into the mid-house cross-aisle, and either descends toward R1–R3 or ascends toward R4–R8. The stage is empty. Black velour closed. The room deliberately shifts register from the immersive neighborhoods they've been walking through — for the first time tonight, they are sitting, and they are looking at a theater.

beats.title: Tour guide solo
beats.body: House goes to black. A single followspot picks up the Tour Guide entering center stage through a gap in the velour. He stands alone on the warm walnut deck in front of the black drape. Solo opening — monologue, song, or hybrid (writer to define). The most intimate moment of the show: one person, one light, 150 people listening. Everything the production has been building toward arrives here.

beats.title: Band arrives · sound layers in
beats.body: One by one, the five band members enter from SL and SR wings. Bassist first — a quiet line under the tour guide. Then drums. Piano. Horns. Each instrument takes an upstage position as a special blooms. The stage picture expands from one to six; the sound gains shape around the solo voice.

beats.title: Poets arrive · voices return
beats.body: Poets from earlier spaces arrive one at a time via different paths — some through the audience aisles, some from the wings, one from upstage through the velour itself. Each gets a spoken moment — a line or fragment carried from their home space — before joining the picture. Language becomes the content. The audience hears voices they recognize from rooms they walked through an hour ago.

tags.label: Band under · poets speak in sequence

beats.title: Dancers arrive · bodies fill the room
beats.body: The dancers arrive last. They enter through the velour — the first time anything has passed through the drape since the tour guide. Their bodies fill the space that words and music have been holding. Movement takes over from language. The band lifts into something more rhythmic. The stage is nearly full; the whole company is gathering.

tags.label: Full wash · movement pickups · velour edge-lit

beats.title: Final number · velour opens · scrim ignites
beats.body: The velour opens. Behind it, the white scrim lights from behind — deep, luminous, seemingly infinite. The stage suddenly has depth. Full ensemble plays, sings, moves. The biggest sound of the show. The biggest picture. This is the only moment in Space 6 where the design does something — everything else has been restraint. The reveal lands because nothing has been revealed before it.

tags.label: Full band + voices · loudest of show

beats.title: Bow · no hierarchy
beats.body: Full company spreads across the 45 ft stage in a single line — tour guide, poets, dancers, musicians — no hierarchy. One bow together. Lights hold for applause, then slowly fade. The scrim stays lit behind them until the last moment, then goes out with everything else. House up. The show is over. Company exits through velour gap; audience egresses via the rear walkway.

tags.label: Hold on applause · slow fade · scrim last

locked: Seating — Option B amphitheater bowl, mid-house entry, 3 rows descending + 5 rows ascending
Stage — 45 × 19 ft at +2 ft, built over dock pit, 6 ft under-stage clear for crossover
Proscenium — matte black frame wrapping existing steel columns, 44 × 24 ft opening
Stage deck — warm walnut hardwood, matte/satin finish
Back-wall system — black velour traveler (12 ft, motorized) + white bobbinet scrim behind for finale reveal
Side masking — standard black legs SL and SR (3 pairs, 14 ft)
House walls — raw brick stays exposed and untreated on both audience sides
Roller door treatment — covered by scenic back wall, not used as show element
Beat arc — 7 beats from tour-guide solo through full-company bow; scrim reveal is the single capital-R reveal
Capacity — 152 positions: 144 fixed + 4 WC + 4 companion at R5 upper and R8 back
Entry/exit — SW corner ramp from Space 5 to mid-house cross-aisle; rear walkway egress after bow
stillOpen: Site survey — measured drawings required; all dimensions best-guess from one photo
Column positions — if inside seating footprint, rows shift; if at stage edges (ideal), wrap as proscenium
Chair specification — theatrical bench vs upholstered fixed seat; espresso leather or walnut ply; must not upstage the room
House lighting plot — pipe grid positions, followspot locations, house wash, aisle lights, emergency egress
Acoustic treatment — absorption on ceiling above audience and rear wall to tame brick/hard-deck reverb
Under-stage program — 675 sq ft of backstage to allocate: crossover, pre-set, quick-change, instrument storage
Scrim reveal mechanics — velour track timing, scrim backlight color/intensity, cue integration with final number
Audience-aisle entry paths for poets — check against sightlines and ADA routes
Tour-guide solo content — monologue, song, or hybrid (director/writer)
Poet fragment selection — which line from each earlier space (writer)
Dance choreography — movement language to be set (choreographer TBD)
